narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.80—well above the typical 3.21 average—and a competitive LSAT score of 37, slightly edging out the average of 36.70, you brought some strong academic credentials to your law school application. Your family falls into the middle income bracket, and you're planning to attend full-time, which is the norm for over 92% of students in the dataset we used. However, the admissions model, trained on past students' data to predict first-time bar exam success, estimated a 61% probability that you would fail the bar on your first try—high enough to trigger an automatic rejection to protect the school's outcomes. The biggest factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, meaning you placed in the bottom tenth of your peers that year, far below the average decile of about 5.5; this stood out as the most influential concern, signaling potential challenges in sustaining performance under pressure. Next, your LSAT score worked in your favor, pulling the prediction toward success more than any other positive element, as it demonstrated strong reasoning skills aligned with bar demands. Your Year 1 decile of 2, also below the average of around 5.7, added a moderate push toward the failure side, suggesting an early pattern of underperforming relative to classmates. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your later-year grades lag significantly behind their mid-pack averages, overshadowing your undergrad strengths despite your full-time commitment. This combination made the model view your profile as riskier than most approved applicants. To move forward, consider retaking courses or seeking targeted tutoring to boost your decile rankings, as improving those could dramatically shift future predictions. You might also explore part-time options or gap-year prep programs proven to build resilience for students like you. Reapplying after addressing these gaps, perhaps with updated transcripts, stands a good chance of flipping the outcome—many in similar spots have succeeded by focusing here. Your LSAT edge is a real asset to build on. Keep pushing; law school's doors aren't closed forever.
